metadata.summary: Modern Python type hints for 3.12+ covering PEP 695 type parameters, PEP 604 union syntax, TypedDict, Protocol, TypeIs/TypeGuard, and gradual rollout strategy. Core rule: annotate the boundary (HTTP, queue, DB) first, then services, then internals. Never silence with bare type: ignore.

content.title: Python Type Hints (Modern 3.12+)
content.summary: Comprehensive type annotation guide for Python 3.12+ covering PEP 695 type parameter syntax, PEP 604 union syntax, TypedDict, Protocol, TypeIs vs TypeGuard, and gradual rollout strategy. Type annotations at API boundaries catch shape mismatches before runtime and give agents accurate generation context. A file-by-file strict rollout is more effective than a repo-wide attempt — the latter produces 1000+ errors and freezes progress.

why.p: Type annotations eliminate entire classes of runtime errors: mismatched dict shapes, None-dereference surprises, function signature contract violations. They are static checks (run mypy, get results in seconds) not runtime overhead. Agents that generate code with type hints produce less broken output because the type checker can verify the implementation against its own type signature.
Modern syntax (PEP 695 generics, PEP 604 unions) is cleaner than pre-2020 typing boilerplate. Python 3.12+ makes it reasonable for domain code, not just libraries.

list.item: Adding type hints to an untyped Python codebase, file-by-file with strict mode on touched files only.
Public API surfaces — service signatures, route handlers, queue consumers, library exports.
Cross-boundary data shapes — request/response, cache values, queue payloads — modelled with TypedDict (cheap) or Pydantic (validating).
Defining structural inter-module contracts without forcing inheritance — Protocol (+ @runtime_checkable if isinstance is needed).
CI gate: type-check files changed in a PR; block regressions on already-typed modules.
Migrating old typing.List / typing.Optional to PEP 585 / PEP 604 syntax via ruff --select UP --fix.
One-off scripts under 100 lines — mypy/pyright setup costs more than it returns.
Heavy metaprogramming surfaces (decorators that mutate signatures, __getattr__ proxies, dynamic ORM-like APIs) — annotations fight the runtime; isolate behind Any or cast.
Codebases pinned to Python 3.8 — this methodology assumes 3.9+ (list[int]) and PEP 604 (3.10+); typing_extensions backports are a last resort.
Pure Pydantic-only domains — adding parallel TypedDict definitions creates two sources of truth for the same shape.
Generated migrations — exclude in mypy config; cluttering them yields no signal.

section.name: syntax-by-python-version
section.p: Typing features were added gradually across Python versions. Before using a feature, verify the codebase's Python floor matches or exceeds the feature's introduction version.

rule.statement: Python 3.9+: list[int], dict[K, V], set[T], tuple[T, ...] (PEP 585). Python 3.10+: X | Y union syntax (PEP 604), ParamSpec, TypeAlias. Python 3.11+: Self, LiteralString, TypeVarTuple, Required/NotRequired. Python 3.12+: PEP 695 class Box[T]:, def first[T]():, type Alias = .... Python 3.13+: TypeIs (PEP 742, bidirectional narrowing), TypeVar defaults, ReadOnly in TypedDict.
rule.rationale: Using X | Y syntax without from __future__ import annotations on Python 3.9 causes TypeError at import time. The feature matrix prevents silent syntax breaks in CI or production.

example.title: PEP 695 generics on Python 3.12+
example.note: PEP 695 eliminates T = TypeVar('T') boilerplate; co-locates type parameters with their scope.

example.title: Legacy TypeVar + Generic
example.note: Verbose; T declaration separated from class; requires Generic base. Migrate to PEP 695.

section.name: union-syntax-and-optional
section.p: Modern Python uses X | Y for unions and X | None for optionals. Avoid Optional[X] and Union[X, Y] in new code. Enable ruff rule UP007 to auto-fix old syntax.

rule.statement: Use X | None, never Optional[X]. Use X | Y, never Union[X, Y]. Enable ruff --select UP007 --fix in pre-commit or CI to enforce this. Never mix Optional and X | None in the same file or codebase.
rule.rationale: X | None is more readable and is PEP 604 standard syntax (3.10+). ruff auto-fixes prevent manual errors. Mixing both confuses readers and frustrates linters.

example.title: PEP 604 union syntax
Mixing Optional and |
example.code:
from typing import Optional, Union
def process(value: Union[int, str]) -> None: # Inconsistent ...
def get_user(user_id: int) -> Optional[dict]: # Inconsistent ...
example.note: Mixes old and new syntax; ruff will flag for auto-fix.

section.name: typeddict-and-protocols
section.p: TypedDict models fixed-shape dicts (cache payloads, queue messages); Protocol models structural interfaces (duck typing without inheritance). Use TypedDict for boundaries; use Protocol for cross-module contracts.

rule.statement: Use class-based TypedDict syntax. Use NotRequired[T] for optional fields (Python 3.11+) instead of total=False on the whole dict. Use ReadOnly[T] (Python 3.13+) for immutable fields. Add @runtime_checkable to Protocol only if isinstance is called; otherwise omit it.
rule.rationale: total=False makes all fields optional (too broad). NotRequired is field-granular and self-documenting. ReadOnly expresses intent without frozen dataclass overhead. @runtime_checkable adds runtime cost; only use if actually needed.

example.title: TypedDict with NotRequired; Protocol structural
example.code:
from typing import TypedDict, NotRequired, ReadOnly, Protocol
class UserProfile(TypedDict): id: ReadOnly[int] # Immutable after creation email: str # Required, mutable bio: NotRequired[str] # Optional field avatar_url: NotRequired[str | None]
class Sendable(Protocol): def send(self, msg: str) -> None: ... def close(self) -> None: ...
# No inheritance needed class EmailClient: def send(self, msg: str) -> None: ... def close(self) -> None: ...
# Type-checks as Sendable without explicit : Sendable def dispatch(client: Sendable) -> None: client.send("hello")
example.note: TypedDict granular + Protocol structural; both readable and type-safe.

example.title: Protocol without @runtime_checkable, but isinstance called
example.code:
class Sendable(Protocol): # No @runtime_checkable def send(self, msg: str) -> None: ...
def dispatch(client: Sendable) -> None: if isinstance(client, Sendable): # TypeError at runtime! client.send("hello")
example.note: isinstance(x, Protocol) without @runtime_checkable raises TypeError. Either add @runtime_checkable or don't isinstance.

section.name: typeis-vs-typeguard
section.p: Both TypeIs and TypeGuard are type narrowing predicates. TypeIs enables bidirectional narrowing (both if and else branches); TypeGuard only narrows the True branch.

rule.statement: Use TypeIs (Python 3.13+) for bidirectional narrowing. Use TypeGuard (3.10+) only if you need pre-3.13 compatibility and the False branch doesn't need narrowing. Pick one style per codebase and document it.
rule.rationale: TypeIs is more powerful (narrows in both branches) and simpler to reason about. TypeGuard is legacy for 3.10-3.12; it requires extra isinstance guards in the False branch, which TypeIs avoids.

example.title: TypeIs bidirectional narrowing
example.note: TypeIs narrows both branches; the else branch automatically infers x: int.

example.title: TypeGuard only narrows True branch
example.note: TypeGuard only narrows if True; the else branch is still int | str (suboptimal).

section.name: gradual-rollout-strategy
section.p: Typing a large codebase at once (mypy --strict repo-wide) produces 1000+ errors and stalls the team. File-by-file adoption with CI gating on changed files is more effective.

rule.statement: Gradual rollout: (1) pick one module, (2) annotate every public signature + module vars, (3) run mypy --strict <file>, (4) fix one error class at a time (start no-untyped-def, then var-annotated, then assignment, then arg-type), (5) add file to mypy.files=[...] allowlist when green, (6) commit. Never run repo-wide --strict on day 1. CI gate: strict-check only files in the diff (git diff --name-only --diff-filter=AM origin/main -- '*.py').
rule.rationale: File-by-file keeps momentum. Allowlisting ensures no regression on typed files. CI gating on touched files prevents coverage drops without blocking on pre-existing untyped code.

example.title: Incremental adoption script
example.code: #!/usr/bin/env bash # scripts/typecheck-touched.sh — strict mypy on changed files only set -euo pipefail BASE_REF="${1:-origin/main}" mapfile -t files < <(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=AM "$BASE_REF" -- '*.py' \ | grep -v -E '^(.*/migrations/|tests/fixtures/|conftest\.py$)') if [[ ${#files[@]} -eq 0 ]]; then echo "No Python files changed."; exit 0 fi echo "Typechecking ${#files[@]} files with mypy --strict..." mypy --strict "${files[@]}"
example.note: Runs strict mypy only on changed files; skips migrations and test fixtures.

section.name: mypy-vs-pyright
section.p: Two major type checkers: mypy (reference implementation, plugin ecosystem) and pyright (Microsoft, 10x faster, IDE integration). Both have strengths; choose based on priorities.

rule.statement: Use pyright in IDE (VSCode Pylance) for fast inline feedback. Use mypy in CI with plugins (django-stubs, pydantic.mypy) for cross-framework validation. Cache .mypy_cache and .pyright_cache in CI keyed on Python + lockfile hash.
rule.rationale: pyright's LSP is faster for interactive editing. mypy's plugin ecosystem handles framework-specific type narrowing (Django Manager.get() returns narrowed types with django-stubs). Both are needed for full coverage.

section.name: common-patterns-and-gotchas
section.p: Type narrowing, forward references, and Protocol runtime semantics are subtle. Understanding these patterns prevents agent errors.

rule.statement: Never use bare # type: ignore; always use # type: ignore[code] with a justification comment (e.g., # type: ignore[misc] # third-party lib missing stubs). Avoid from __future__ import annotations with Pydantic v1 (breaks body parsing). Remember Generic[T] has no runtime enforcement (Repository[int] and Repository[str] are the same class). Use Self (3.11+) for builder return types, not TForward = TypeVar(..., bound="Foo").
rule.rationale: Bare # type: ignore hides future bugs; ruff PGH003 blocks it. from __future__ import annotations + Pydantic v1 causes "field type is undefined" errors at runtime. Generic[T] is purely static; don't rely on it for runtime type safety. Self is the standard pattern for fluent builders.

example.title: Justified type: ignore
example.code:
import some_untyped_lib
# type: ignore[import-not-found] — some_untyped_lib lacks stubs, no viable fix result: dict = some_untyped_lib.do_something()
example.note: Code + comment explains why the suppress is necessary.

example.title: Bare # type: ignore and Generic[T] confusion
example.code:
# Bad: bare type: ignore x: int = some_func() # type: ignore
# Bad: relying on Generic[T] at runtime class Repository[T]: def __init__(self, id: int) -> None: self.id = id
r1: Repository[int] = Repository(1) r2: Repository[str] = Repository(1) assert type(r1) is type(r2) # True! Generic[T] is no-op at runtime
example.note: Bare type: ignore hides reason; Generic[T] offers no runtime distinction.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents adding type hints should follow a structured workflow: one file at a time, strict mypy on touched code, CI gating on changes, no silent errors.

rule.statement: Agent workflow: (1) pick one module, (2) annotate all public signatures + module-level vars, (3) run mypy --strict <file>, (4) fix errors in priority order (no-untyped-def → var-annotated → assignment → arg-type), (5) add file to mypy.files allowlist when 0 errors, (6) commit. If mypy error output is verbose, paste verbatim instead of summarizing; never ignore 'Revealed type' or stderr messages.
rule.rationale: Structured workflow prevents random exploration. Priority ordering ensures high-value annotations first. Verbatim error output lets the human debug when needed. Allowlist enforcement prevents regression.

template.purpose: Strict mypy on changed files only — CI/pre-push script.
[tool.mypy] in pyproject.toml with strict + per-file overrides for tests.
Protocol structural subtyping with and without @runtime_checkable.
PEP 695 generic class, function, and constrained type parameters.
